brand.name: Alex and Ani
brand.price_range: $22–$88 core range; sale items from $4.90; premium sets up to $150
brand.materials: recycled copper and eco-conscious metals, Rafaelian gold/silver/rose gold finishes, made in Rhode Island
brand.tone: uplifting, empowering, spiritually warm — affirmation-driven with celebratory and inclusive messaging
brand.usp: Patented expandable one-size-fits-all bangles made from recycled metals in Rhode Island, backed by a 100+ symbol library and a Charity by Design program that has donated $20M+ to partner causes.

competitors.name: Pandora
competitors.why: BOGO stacking mechanics and charm tour Reels directly compete with Alex and Ani's stackable bangle system and milestone gifting positioning.

competitors.name: Kendra Scott
competitors.why: Micro-influencer UGC Reels (+175% ROAS YoY) and personalization story at accessible price points overlap heavily with Alex and Ani's core audience and gifting angle.

competitors.name: BaubleBar
competitors.why: Aggressive doorbuster pricing and creator try-on Reels with promo codes compete for the Gen Z price-sensitive segment Alex and Ani is failing to convert through paid.

competitors.name: Gorjana
competitors.why: UGC haul Reels with creator discount codes and female athlete ambassador films drive brand affinity in the same 22–40 female lifestyle audience.

competitors.name: Mejuri
competitors.why: Lo-fi UGC micro-influencer Reels dropped CAC from $60+ to sub-$40 and drove 1.7x conversion lift — a direct proof-of-concept for the UGC gap Alex and Ani has not yet filled in paid social.

ad_formats.type: Dynamic Product Ads (DPA) with Afterpay overlay
ad_formats.description: Enriched catalog ads auto-populated with product imagery and Afterpay installment messaging overlaid at the bottom; performance-driven retargeting format.
ALL-CAPS bold typography announcing percentage-off discounts on solid color backgrounds; zero product photography present in the creative.

ad_formats.type: Lifestyle TV and video — cinematic emotional spots
ad_formats.description: Golden-hour, warm-toned brand films with voiceover posing questions like 'What Does Love Look Like?' featuring stacked bangles on wrists in real moments; currently underused in paid social.

ad_formats.type: Symbol and Product of the Month static
ad_formats.description: Monthly recurring single-charm feature with the charm photographed in isolation on a clean background; gifting-occasion copy paired with the product name and price.
Holiday color-coded static ads featuring a free item threshold offer; product imagery of bundled pieces laid flat with GWP callout text overlay.

brand_ad_audit.visual_style: Clean minimal layouts on white or warm neutral backgrounds; symbol-driven charm imagery in Rafaelian gold, silver, and rose gold; stacked bracelet lifestyle photography with natural golden-hour lighting; restrained typographic hierarchy using serif or softened sans-serif fonts.
hooks: Emotional question opener — 'What does love look like?' or 'What does it mean to you?' framing the charm as a vessel for personal meaning.
Discount urgency announcement — ALL-CAPS typography leading with the percentage off before any product is introduced.
Product-of-the-month gifting prompt — 'This month's must-have' or occasion anchor like 'Perfect for Valentine's Day' over a single isolated charm image.
strategy_gaps: Zero paid UGC amplification despite 975K Instagram followers and an active organic TikTok nostalgia revival — the highest-performing format for all five key competitors is entirely absent from paid rotation.

Sale creative contains no product imagery — all promotional ads are pure typographic banners, while every major competitor leads with product in their discount creative, causing Alex and Ani to lose purchase intent at the consideration stage.

The brand's most defensible differentiators — American-made manufacturing, recycled metals, the Charity by Design program, and the 100+ symbol meaning library — never appear in paid social creative, leaving competitive positioning entirely unclaimed.

ad_types.description: A creator holds their Pandora bracelet up to camera and walks through each charm, naming the memory or milestone each one represents; warm natural lighting, handheld intimate feel, 15–30 seconds.
ad_types.why_it_works: The charm-by-charm tour format converts passive viewers into active purchasers by triggering personal memory association with each item shown — each charm becomes a buying cue.

ad_types.description: Multi-frame carousel opening with the BOGO mechanic prominently displayed, followed by slides pairing specific charms that 'go together' — spring themed, milestone themed — with price callouts per pair.
ad_types.why_it_works: The stacking loop mechanic turns a discount into a curated collection-building prompt, increasing average order value while the sale framing lowers the perceived barrier to buying multiple pieces.

ad_types.description: 30–60 second cinematic film featuring a celebrity or high-reach creator receiving a Pandora gift tied to a milestone; slow motion close-ups on the clasp and charm details, ambient music swells.
ad_types.why_it_works: Celebrity association and milestone emotion elevate the brand above accessory-price perception, sustaining premium positioning even during promotional periods.

ad_types.description: Each carousel frame features a single charm or set with the price and a gifting occasion label — Birthday, Graduation, Just Because — organized under the $50 threshold banner.
ad_types.why_it_works: Decision simplification at the gifting occasion removes buyer hesitation for gift-givers who lack jewelry knowledge but have a clear budget ceiling.

competitor_ads.visual_style: High-fashion editorial with clean white and champagne gold backgrounds; sharp professional lighting with selective bokeh on charms; polished typography in Pandora's proprietary font; occasional warm-toned lifestyle inserts.
hooks: Emotional milestone anchor — opening frame names a life event (graduation, new baby, first job) before revealing the charm.
Nostalgia revival self-talk — creator-voice opener: 'I need to bring back my Pandora bracelet and here is why.' — one video generated $683K MIV.
Urgency and scarcity — 'Only available through [date]' or 'Limited charm release' framing before showing the product.

ad_types.type: Micro-influencer UGC Reel with promo code
ad_types.description: Everyday creators (10K–100K followers) film themselves unboxing or styling Kendra Scott pieces in their own homes; direct-to-camera delivery with a unique discount code displayed as text overlay at the end.
ad_types.why_it_works: Peer-level trust removes the 'ad skepticism' filter; promo codes create trackable attribution and personal urgency ('this is my code, it expires') that polished brand ads cannot replicate.

ad_types.description: Bright yellow background with bold black and white typography announcing the Kendra Scott branded sale event name, date, and discount tier; product imagery inset or absent, brand color owns the frame.
ad_types.why_it_works: Branded sale event naming creates cultural belonging — shoppers feel they are part of an exclusive moment, not just receiving a generic discount.

ad_types.description: A man is shown looking confused browsing online, then a Kendra Scott gift guide appears framed as the solution; voiceover addresses the male viewer directly with reassuring, simple gift advice.
ad_types.why_it_works: Directly addresses the overlooked male gift buyer segment with empathy and simplicity — converts a high-intent but low-confidence shopper who would otherwise abandon.

ad_types.description: Short punchy Reel revealing a licensed collab piece (Barbie, in their case) with a branded reveal moment — product emerging from packaging or a thematic unbox — ending with a shop link.
ad_types.why_it_works: IP nostalgia extends reach beyond the core jewelry audience to fans of the licensed property, driving discovery impressions at lower CPMs than category-targeted campaigns.

competitor_ads.visual_style: Product-forward on brand-yellow or soft neutral backgrounds; natural studio lighting with lifestyle inserts; clean bold typography with Kendra Scott's signature font; UGC content intentionally lo-fi with natural home environments.
hooks: Birthday personal trigger — 'Your birthday is coming up and you deserve this' or 'Get yourself something for your birthday.'
Branded urgency — 'Wake up, it is Yellow Friday' opening frame that names the proprietary sale event before any product is shown.
Gifting problem opener — humorous scenario of a man clueless about jewelry gifts, followed by the solution reveal.

ad_types.description: Bold red or black background with oversized price callout — '$10 Doorbuster' or 'Only 2 Left' — paired with a single product image; stripped-back design maximizing the discount message.
ad_types.why_it_works: Extreme price clarity eliminates purchase hesitation at the top of the funnel — the viewer does not need to read copy, the discount is the visual.

ad_types.type: UGC creator try-on Reel with promo code
ad_types.description: Creator films themselves in a try-on haul format, placing BaubleBar pieces on their wrist or neck on camera, rotating to show different angles; unique promo code displayed as a persistent text overlay.
ad_types.why_it_works: Real-time try-on creates vicarious product experience — the viewer imagines the product on themselves rather than on a model, closing the gap between inspiration and purchase.

ad_types.description: Short Reel showing the step-by-step process of selecting a personalized piece — name, initial, or design — with close-up footage of the finished product being revealed at the end.
ad_types.why_it_works: Process revelation satisfies the 'how does this work?' objection that stops personalizable product browsers from converting, turning curiosity into confidence.

ad_types.description: Timelapse or quick-cut sequence of a wrist being styled one piece at a time, building a full stack; each added piece is called out with a price tag overlay; final frame shows the complete look.
ad_types.why_it_works: Progressive stacking makes each piece feel like a building block rather than a standalone purchase — increases basket size by framing completeness as the goal.

competitor_ads.visual_style: Punchy, high-contrast backgrounds in brand red, black, or pastels; direct product focus with minimal lifestyle context; text-heavy with price callouts; UGC content raw and handheld without color correction.
hooks: Hard discount opener — oversized price or percentage callout fills the first frame before any product context is given.
Creator direct-to-camera trust opener — creator looks into lens and says 'I found the best deal on jewelry right now and I need to show you.'
Personalization process opener — close-up of hands selecting letters or charms before the finished piece is revealed.

ad_types.description: 45–90 second cinematic film featuring a female athlete in her element — training, competing, or quiet moments post-game — with Gorjana pieces visible; no hard sell, brand ethos carried by the athlete's identity.
ad_types.why_it_works: Identity association rather than product demonstration — the viewer adopts the brand by associating with the ambassador's values, driving a 45% documented sales lift.

ad_types.type: UGC haul Reel with creator discount code
ad_types.description: Creator films a haul of Gorjana pieces received in a PR or affiliate package — unboxing, trying on, commenting on quality and feel — with a personal discount code shown at the end; 23+ active codes running simultaneously.
ad_types.why_it_works: Volume of active creator codes creates a persistent low-CPM discovery layer — any viewer who engages with jewelry content has a high probability of encountering a Gorjana code from a peer-level source.

ad_types.description: Minimal lifestyle image with a clean overlay announcing a rare or time-limited sale window — 'We almost never do this' framing — with a countdown or date callout; product visible but secondary to the scarcity message.
ad_types.why_it_works: Gorjana's infrequent sale posture makes each discount feel genuinely exclusive rather than routine — FOMO is credible because the audience knows it is rare.

ad_types.description: 15–30 second no-voiceover Reel of jewelry in motion — wrist flip, hand through hair, walking through soft light — set to trending audio; brand name appears at the end only.
ad_types.why_it_works: No-sell content builds aesthetic aspiration and brand recall without triggering ad fatigue, keeping CPMs low and organic reach high on Reels algorithm.

competitor_ads.visual_style: Warm coastal California aesthetic — natural light, sun-bleached backgrounds, sandy textures; muted warm palette in ivory, sand, gold, and blush; minimal text; cinematic crop ratios even in short-form content.
hooks: Identity aesthetic match — first frame is a lifestyle shot so visually cohesive with the creator's feed that it reads as organic content before any product is shown.
No-voiceover visual hook — product in motion with trending audio immediately, no spoken introduction.
Rare-sale scarcity opener — 'We almost never do this' or 'This is your one chance this season' before any product or price is revealed.

ad_types.description: Micro-influencers (under 50K) film casual at-home or on-street content wearing Mejuri pieces; natural imperfect lighting, no scripts, creator speaks directly to camera or captions replace voiceover; drove CAC from $60+ to sub-$40.
ad_types.why_it_works: Authenticity signals at scale — low production cost means high volume, and the peer-level delivery consistently outperforms polished brand creative for conversion at the bottom of funnel.

ad_types.description: Extreme close-up of a wrist or neck with jewelry in frame, photographed in natural window light with minimal editing to look organic; styled to match editorial Instagram aesthetics rather than brand ad aesthetics.
ad_types.why_it_works: Looks like organic content in feed — lower ad detection by viewers means higher dwell time and click-through before the viewer realizes it is a paid placement.

ad_types.description: Swipeable carousel where each slide features a different stacking combination of Mejuri pieces, styled on-model; final slide is a shop-the-look CTA with all items linked.
ad_types.why_it_works: Progressive discovery mechanic — each swipe deepens engagement and introduces additional SKUs, increasing the probability of a multi-item cart.

ad_types.description: Short Reel using a trending audio format — Girl Math, Get Ready With Me, Day in My Life — to frame a Mejuri purchase decision; conversational and self-aware copy paired with product footage.
ad_types.why_it_works: Trend-adjacent content inherits discoverability from the trending format's existing reach — Mejuri's 'Girl Math' Reel drove 120 fan-made response videos in one week and cut CAC by 35%.

competitor_ads.visual_style: Aspirational lo-fi — natural light with slight overexposure on skin and metal; muted warm neutrals and soft whites; editorial framing in casual settings (coffee shops, home kitchens, streetwear); minimal branded text overlays to preserve organic feel.
hooks: Self-treat permission opener — 'You have been working hard and you deserve this' or 'Treat yourself, no occasion needed' before any product is shown.
Trend-hijack copy hook — 'Girl Math is buying yourself jewelry during a sale because you are saving money' spoken to camera.
Stack challenge UGC opener — creator holds up a bare wrist to camera and says 'Build my stack with me' before adding pieces one by one.

trending_formats.description: A creator or everyday person holds their stacked bracelet or charm bracelet up to camera and narrates the personal meaning behind each individual charm — milestone, memory, or symbol — in 15–30 seconds. The format is intimate, handheld, and conversational.

Swipeable carousel organized under a clear price ceiling (Under $30, Under $50, Under $100) with each slide featuring one product, its name, price, and a one-line gifting occasion label. Final slide is a CTA with shop link.

trending_formats.format: Lo-fi UGC haul Reel with creator promo code
trending_formats.description: Micro or nano creator films a casual unboxing or try-on of multiple pieces from a brand in their own space — bedroom, kitchen, car — with natural lighting and a conversational delivery. A unique promo code appears as a text overlay and in caption.

A wrist begins bare on camera and pieces are added one at a time in quick cuts or timelapse, each with a price tag overlay, building to a complete stacked look. Final frame shows the full stack with a total value and sale price callout.

Creator uses a trending audio format or spoken meme structure (Girl Math, Things That Are Worth It, Unpopular Opinion) to frame a brand purchase decision in a self-aware, humorous way that rides existing algorithmic momentum.

content_types.name: UGC Nostalgia Reel Series
content_types.hook: Lo-fi creator content that rides the organic TikTok nostalgia wave for Alex and Ani with paid amplification — real people, real stacks, real meaning.
content_types.why_it_works: Mejuri's lo-fi UGC micro-influencer Reels cut CAC from $60+ to sub-$40 and delivered 1.7x conversion lift. Gorjana runs 23+ simultaneous creator codes. Alex and Ani has 975K Instagram followers and an active organic nostalgia wave on TikTok that is generating reach without paid support — amplifying this through paid is the single fastest lever available. No UGC exists in their current paid rotation, meaning this is a zero-competition internal category with proven category-wide ROI.

ads.title: Bring Back My Stack

storyboard.visual: Vertical handheld shot. Creator (woman, late 20s, casual home setting — morning light, kitchen counter background) holds her bare wrist up to the camera and looks directly into the lens. Expression is warm and nostalgic, slight smile. Shot is slightly underexposed and natural — no ring light.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover spoken naturally, no script feel: 'Okay I need to talk about something. I found my Alex and Ani bangles from like 2017 in a box.' Trending nostalgic audio (low volume underneath — something in the category of soft indie or early-2010s pop instrumental).

storyboard.visual: Cut to close-up of a small box being opened on a wooden table — inside are 4–5 original Alex and Ani bangles: a zodiac bangle, an initial charm bangle, a path of life bangle, and a beaded bangle. Creator's hands (no manicure, real person) lift each one out gently. Natural window light catches the Rafaelian gold finish.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover continues: 'I forgot how much I loved these. They still look so good.' Text overlay in lowercase white thin font bottom-left: 'alex and ani nostalgia hits different'

storyboard.visual: Creator slides each bangle onto her wrist one at a time in a continuous close-up shot — camera stays tight on the wrist. The expandable mechanism is visible as she slides each one. Stack builds from 1 to 5 bangles. She rotates her wrist slowly at the end so the stack catches the light.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover: 'The sliding mechanism still works perfectly. And they are still on sale right now — some starting at under $25.' Text overlay appears with each bangle added: name of bangle + sale price (e.g., 'Zodiac Bangle — $28 now').

storyboard.visual: Creator faces camera again, wrist raised showing full stack, grinning. She points at the stack with her other hand. Behind her, casual home setting. Shot is warm and slightly grainy — authentic.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover: 'The sale is on right now. I will link everything below. Use my code for extra off.' Bold text overlay center screen: '[CREATORCODE] for extra 15% off' in Alex and Ani gold (#ab8c52) on dark background bar. URL shown underneath.

ads.copy: Found my Alex and Ani from 2017 — they still slap. Sale is live. Stack starts under $25.
ads.reference: Adapted from Mejuri's lo-fi UGC micro-influencer Reel format that drove CAC from $60+ to sub-$40. Translated from Mejuri's 'self-treat' framing into Alex and Ani's nostalgia revival moment — the hook is personal rediscovery rather than self-permission, which is more authentic to the organic TikTok conversation already happening around this brand.
negative_prompts: No ring lighting or studio-quality lighting — the entire value of this format is its organic, real-person feel; polished lighting immediately signals paid ad and kills trust.
No manicured hands or styled nails — casting must use a real-person creator whose hands look everyday, not editorial.
No branded lower-thirds or Alex and Ani logo watermarks in the first 3 seconds — the hook must read as organic content before the viewer's ad-detection activates.
No trending audio that is overused to the point of parody — music selection must feel current and low-key, not ironic or meme-tier.
Avoid showing the bangles flat on a surface in the opening scene — the reveal must happen on a real wrist to maintain the personal nostalgia narrative.
ads.post_production_notes: Color grade should be minimal — slight warmth (+10 temperature), no saturation boost, slight vignette to feel phone-native. Cut on the natural rhythm of the voiceover, not to a beat. Text overlays should use a thin lowercase system font (not Alex and Ani's brand serif) to preserve the UGC aesthetic — the brand font only appears in the promo code bar in scene 4. Audio mix: voiceover at 100%, background music at 15–20% to keep it subliminal. No transitions — hard cuts only between scenes 2 and 3. The creator code bar in scene 4 should animate in with a simple fade (0.3s), not a branded motion graphic.

ads.title: My Charm Means This

storyboard.visual: Extreme close-up on a wrist wearing three Alex and Ani bangles — the Path of Life Charm Bangle, the Zodiac Charm Bangle (Scorpio), and the Infinite Karma Wrap Bangle. The wrist rotates slowly in soft natural window light. No person visible yet, just the jewelry in motion. Shot is handheld, slightly unsteady.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay appears in thin white lowercase font: 'every charm means something' — stays on screen for 2 seconds then dissolves. Background audio: quiet ambient or lo-fi instrumental, trending but not recognizable.

storyboard.visual: Cut to creator (woman, early 30s) looking at her wrist, then at camera. She touches the Path of Life bangle with one finger. Casual setting — a coffee shop or bright home interior. Shot is medium close-up, handheld, portrait orientation.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Creator speaks directly to camera: 'This one is my Path of Life bangle. I got it when I moved to a new city alone. It reminded me I was exactly where I was supposed to be.' Text overlay (bottom third, white, small): 'Path of Life Charm Bangle — $32'

storyboard.visual: Creator holds up wrist again and touches the Zodiac bangle next. Smile is genuine. She glances at it before looking back at the lens.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Creator continues: 'And this is my Scorpio bangle. My mom got it for me. I have worn it every day for three years.' Text overlay updates: 'Zodiac Charm Bangle — $38 | Sale starts at $22 right now'

storyboard.visual: Wide pull-back reveals creator sitting at a table with three more Alex and Ani bangles laid out in front of her, including the Initial Charm Bangle and Starfish Charity Bangle. She picks up the Starfish bangle and holds it toward the camera.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Creator: 'This one donates 20% to ocean conservation. I did not even know that when I bought it. Now I buy these as gifts because they actually mean something.' Bold text overlay: 'Sale on now. Bangles from $22.' CTA button graphic: 'Shop the Story'

ads.copy: Every charm has a story. Right now those stories start at $22. Which one is yours?
ads.reference: Adapted from Pandora's influencer charm tour Reel format — the walk-through-each-charm structure with personal memory narration. Translated away from Pandora's polished editorial delivery into a lo-fi, direct-to-camera UGC register that matches Alex and Ani's warmer, more personal brand voice. The critical addition is the meaning narrative — each charm gets a story, not just a name — which directly addresses the competitor gap of unclaimed symbolism in paid social.
negative_prompts: Do not use a professional model or anyone who reads as 'influencer-polished' — casting must be an authentic everyday person whose credibility comes from warmth and relatability, not aspirational appearance.
Avoid showing all bangles at once in an organized flat lay in the first scene — the charm-by-charm reveal structure requires sequential discovery to maintain the tour narrative.
No upbeat pop or energetic music — the audio bed must be quiet and contemplative to match the emotional tone of personal meaning; high-energy music will undercut the sincerity.
Do not add motion graphics or branded lower-thirds to the charm name overlays — simple white text on the actual frame only, to preserve the UGC register.
Avoid the word 'discount' anywhere in the voiceover — the sale mechanic lives only in the text overlay, keeping the creator's spoken narrative purely about meaning and story.
ads.post_production_notes: Color grade: warm golden tone with slight lift in shadows to keep faces readable; aim for the aesthetic of natural window-light photography, not corrected studio footage. Cuts should fall on natural pauses in the creator's speech — do not cut mid-sentence. The product name and price text overlays should appear 0.5 seconds after the creator begins speaking about each piece, not simultaneously — this preserves the conversational feel. End card (final 1.5 seconds) is a static frame of the full bracelet stack on the table with the sale CTA over a semi-transparent dark bar at the bottom. No music sync cuts needed — audio is ambient throughout.

content_types.name: Product-Forward Sale Carousel
content_types.hook: Swipeable price-tier gift guides that put product front and center in every sale frame — replacing the current typographic-only discount banners with visual shopping moments.
content_types.why_it_works: Pandora runs Gifts Under $50 carousels. Kendra Scott runs price-sorted discovery carousels. BaubleBar layers price callouts on every product frame. Alex and Ani's own sale ads are pure typography with zero product imagery — they are giving competitor carousels a free conversion opportunity against their own promotional spend. Pandora's price-sorted carousels are one of the most documented high-ROAS formats in the accessible jewelry category. This content type directly addresses the most critical identified gap.

ads.title: Gifts Under $30 — The Charm Guide

storyboard.visual: Cover slide. Warm cream background. Two stacked bangles photographed in a close overhead shot — the Initial Charm Bangle and the Sentiment Bead Bracelet — resting on a piece of linen fabric. No hands. Large bold serif headline text centered: 'GIFTS UNDER $30' in Alex and Ani gold (#ab8c52). Subline beneath in thin sans-serif: 'Sale on now — meaningful pieces that actually matter.' Small 'Swipe to explore' indicator bottom right.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'GIFTS UNDER $30 — Swipe to find yours'

storyboard.visual: Slide 2. Single product hero: Sentiment Bead Bracelet ($22) photographed on a woman's wrist against a soft white background. Wrist is turned so the affirmation word bead faces camera. Clean natural light. Bottom of frame: product name in thin uppercase sans-serif 'SENTIMENT BEAD BRACELET', price '$22' in large bold gold text, and a one-line occasion label in italic serif: 'For the friend who needs to hear it.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'SENTIMENT BEAD BRACELET — $22 | For the friend who needs to hear it.'

storyboard.visual: Slide 3. Single product hero: Initial Charm Bangle ($28) — letter 'M' shown — photographed flat on a warm wooden surface in raking natural light that catches the Rafaelian gold. Bottom of frame: 'INITIAL CHARM BANGLE', '$28', italic label: 'For the person you know everything about.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'INITIAL CHARM BANGLE — $28 | For the person you know everything about.'

storyboard.visual: Slide 4. Single product hero: Rose Gold Feather Charm Bangle ($30) photographed on a wrist with two other bangles stacked, but blurred in background — the feather bangle in sharp focus in foreground. Soft pink background tones. Bottom of frame: 'ROSE GOLD FEATHER BANGLE', '$30 — Sale from $22', italic label: 'For the one who is always becoming.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'ROSE GOLD FEATHER BANGLE — $30 | For the one who is always becoming.'

storyboard.visual: Final CTA slide. Warm cream background matching cover. Four of the featured bangles arranged in a loose cluster photographed overhead — not perfectly styled, slightly organic placement. Centered text: 'Sale ends soon.' Large gold CTA button graphic: 'SHOP GIFTS UNDER $30'. Small subline: 'Free shipping on orders over $50. Made in Rhode Island.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'Shop Gifts Under $30 — Sale ends soon. Free shipping over $50.'

ads.copy: Meaningful gifts under $30. Bangles that say something real — without spending a lot. Sale on now.
ads.reference: Directly adapted from Pandora's Gifts Under $50 price-sorted carousel and Kendra Scott's price-tier gifting carousels. The key adaptation for Alex and Ani is the addition of a symbolic occasion label on each product slide — 'For the one who is always becoming' — which activates the brand's meaning narrative within the promotional format rather than running a pure price-discount grid. This differentiates from competitor carousels while preserving the decision-simplification mechanic.
negative_prompts: Do not use a pure white background on product slides — the warm cream and linen textures are essential to maintain Alex and Ani's aesthetic distinction from Pandora's clinical white editorial style.
Avoid showing more than one product per slide after the cover — multi-product slides fragment attention and reduce the individual purchase intention each frame needs to generate.
No stock-photo hands or generic model wrists — all wrist shots must be cast specifically for skin tone diversity to reflect the brand's inclusive positioning.
Do not use a countdown timer graphic — urgency should come from copy ('Sale ends soon') not from digital countdown mechanics that feel cheap and inconsistent with the brand's spiritual warmth.
Avoid bold red or orange sale color coding — all sale callouts must remain within the Alex and Ani gold, silver, and cream palette to protect brand equity during the promotional window.
ads.post_production_notes: All slides must maintain a consistent color temperature (warm, approximately 5200K equivalent) — do not allow individual product shots to vary in white balance across the carousel, as inconsistency breaks the editorial cohesion. Product photography retouching should be light — remove dust and fingerprints but preserve natural metal texture and patina. The gold price text (#ab8c52) should be tested at both 100% opacity and 85% opacity against cream backgrounds for legibility. Carousel cover slide should be produced in both 1:1 (Instagram Feed) and 4:5 (Meta Feed) crop ratios. For Story adaptation, each slide becomes a full-screen 9:16 frame with the product centered and text repositioned to the top third to avoid thumb coverage.

ads.title: Stack More, Spend Less — The Sale Carousel

storyboard.visual: Cover slide. Black background (rare for Alex and Ani — signals urgency/event status). A wrist wearing a full stack of 6 Alex and Ani bangles in gold, silver, and rose gold is photographed in dramatic side lighting against the dark background. The stack glows. Centered white serif headline: 'SALE IS ON.' Subline in gold thin uppercase: 'Build your stack. Spend less. Start at $22.' Swipe indicator bottom right.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'SALE IS ON — Bangles from $22. Swipe to build your stack.'

storyboard.visual: Slide 2 — Stack Level 1: 'The Starter.' Single bangle on a wrist: Sentiment Bead Bracelet ($22, on sale). Clean cream background returns. Wrist at 45 degrees, natural light. Text bottom of frame: 'STARTER STACK — 1 bangle' in small uppercase, '$22' bold gold, below it: 'Add one. Start your story.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'STARTER STACK — Sentiment Bead Bracelet | $22'

storyboard.visual: Slide 3 — Stack Level 2: 'The Pair.' Same wrist now with two bangles added: the Sentiment Bead Bracelet plus the Initial Charm Bangle. The second bangle is being slid on mid-frame — motion captured mid-slide. Text: 'ADD A SECOND — 2 bangles' '$50 total', below: 'Because your initial should be in the stack.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'ADD A SECOND — Initial Charm Bangle | Stack total: $50'

storyboard.visual: Slide 4 — Stack Level 3: 'The Full Story.' Wrist now wearing the original two plus the Path of Life Charm Bangle and the Zodiac Charm Bangle — four bangles total, shot from above with the wrist resting on a linen surface. Text: 'THE FULL STACK — 4 bangles' '$116 total' with a strikethrough at '$148 regular' above it in lighter grey. Subline: 'Every piece means something different.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'THE FULL STACK — 4 bangles | $116 (save $32 at sale price)'

storyboard.visual: Final CTA slide. Black background returns matching the cover. The full 6-bangle stack from the cover image appears again, but now each bangle has a small white label pointing to it with the bangle name. Centered text in white: 'Sale ends [date].' Gold CTA bar: 'SHOP THE STACK — Up to 30% Off'. Small fine print line: 'Free shipping over $50. Eco-conscious metals. Made in Rhode Island.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'Shop the Stack — Up to 30% Off. Sale ends soon.'

ads.copy: Build your stack during our sale. Every bangle tells a different story. Start at $22 — go as deep as you want.
ads.reference: Adapted from BaubleBar's layer-by-layer stack reveal Reel and Pandora's BOGO stacking carousel format. The key translation for Alex and Ani is the 'Stack Level' progression structure — framing each product addition as a storytelling escalation rather than a pure volume discount, which preserves brand meaning while delivering the stacking mechanic. The black cover slide is a deliberate departure from Alex and Ani's usual palette to signal a sale event without using the competitor-standard red, consistent with Kendra Scott's Yellow Friday branded event approach.
negative_prompts: Do not use AI-generated hands or jewelry renders — the physical expandable mechanism sliding onto a real wrist is a core product demonstration moment that must be captured practically.
Avoid showing the bangles in a flat lay in any slide after the cover — every product frame must be on-wrist to reinforce the 'build your stack' behavioral narrative.
No red or orange sale color accents anywhere — the black-and-gold palette established in this concept must hold across all slides; introducing competitor sale colors dilutes the branded event feel.
Avoid pricing callouts that include the word 'discount' — use 'sale price' or simply the dollar amount alongside the regular strikethrough price; 'discount' language cheapens the brand register.
Do not add animation or motion to the static carousel — this format is designed for Meta Feed where carousels perform as statics; animated overlays will not render correctly across all placements.
ads.post_production_notes: The black-background slides (cover and final CTA) should be photographed on the same day as the cream-background product slides so that wrist casting remains consistent across the carousel — the same hand/arm must appear in all slides for visual continuity. Color grade the black slides with a slightly warm black (not pure RGB black) to prevent a harsh cold contrast with the gold jewelry. On cream slides, match the warm white balance used across the Gifts Under $30 carousel for cross-campaign visual consistency. The strikethrough regular price in slide 4 should be rendered in a light warm grey (not red) — thin weight, clearly readable but not visually dominant. Final CTA slide label lines (pointing to each bangle) should be clean hairline weight in white — no arrowheads, just fine connector lines.

content_types.name: Meaning-Driven Emotional Reel
content_types.hook: Short emotional Reels built around the symbolism of specific charms — making the meaning narrative the reason to buy during a sale, not just the reason to love the brand.
content_types.why_it_works: Alex and Ani's most defensible differentiator — a 100+ symbol library with documented meaning behind every design — does not exist in any current paid social creative. Pandora's charm tour Reels generate memory-association purchasing. Mejuri's 'Girl Math' trend-jacking Reels drive bottom-of-funnel conversion through emotional reframing. Alex and Ani has a stronger symbolism story than either competitor, but it runs no paid content communicating it. This content type directly claims the unclaimed territory and gives the sale campaign emotional substance beyond price.

ads.title: The Path of Life — Why I Bought This During a Sale

storyboard.visual: Extreme close-up macro shot of the Path of Life Charm Bangle — the spiral path symbol in Rafaelian gold filling the frame. The bangle slowly rotates on a wrist against a blurred warm background (golden hour light through a window). Text appears in thin white serif font, centered: 'What does this symbol mean?'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Ambient tone, very quiet. Text overlay fades in over 1 second: 'What does this symbol mean?' No voiceover in first 3 seconds — visual hook only.

storyboard.visual: Cut to a woman (30s, naturally lit, close-up medium shot, casual clothing — linen or soft cotton) sitting by a window looking at her wrist. She lifts the Path of Life bangle with two fingers and turns it toward the light. She looks at camera and begins to speak. Behind her is soft, slightly out-of-focus home interior.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover / direct to camera: 'The Path of Life symbol means your journey is exactly where it is supposed to be. Even when it feels like you are going in circles, you are actually spiraling upward.' Gentle piano or acoustic ambient music, low in the mix.

storyboard.visual: Cut to close-up of the charm itself again — this time photographer catches the light on the spiral in a slow horizontal slide across the charm face. The metal texture is visible. The camera movement is deliberate and calm.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover continues: 'I bought this during a sale two years ago. I almost did not. Now I wear it every single day.' Text overlay appears at bottom in gold italic serif: 'Path of Life Charm Bangle — Eco-conscious recycled metals. Made in Rhode Island.'

storyboard.visual: Cut back to woman on camera. She smiles and looks at her wrist one more time. She says the final line to camera. The shot then cuts to a clean product shot — the bangle on a cream linen surface in raking light — with a sale price overlay appearing.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover: 'It is on sale right now. If you have been thinking about it — this is the sign.' Cut to product shot. Text overlay: 'PATH OF LIFE CHARM BANGLE — $32 | On Sale Now' with CTA button: 'Shop the Symbol'

ads.copy: The spiral means you are exactly where you are supposed to be. This is the sign to get the one you have been thinking about. On sale now.
ads.reference: Adapted from Pandora's emotional milestone anchor hook and Mejuri's self-treat permission opener, combined with Alex and Ani's own cinematic brand film aesthetic ('What Does Love Look Like?'). The critical adaptation is treating the sale as the 'sign' — reframing the discount as a meaningful cosmic prompt rather than a commercial event, which is entirely native to Alex and Ani's spiritual brand voice and has no equivalent in competitor paid creative.
negative_prompts: Do not use any sale countdown timers or urgency graphics — the emotional register of this concept depends on the sale feeling like a meaningful moment, not a commercial pressure tactic.
Avoid celebrity or model-tier casting — the person on camera must be a relatable everyday woman whose credibility is warmth and authenticity, not beauty-standard aspiration.
No high-energy music or trending audio — the audio bed must be contemplative and quiet; even a low-volume pop instrumental will undercut the spiritual tone.

Avoid the macro close-up shot being AI-generated or digitally rendered — the physical texture of the recycled metal and the charm patina are core to the 'made with intention' visual proof; renders look synthetic.
Do not show any competitor products or generic charm bracelets in the background of any shot — styling of the background must be intentional and jewelry-clean.
ads.post_production_notes: The macro charm close-up in scenes 1 and 3 should be shot on a 100mm macro lens at f/2.8 or wider to achieve the shallow depth of field that makes the spiral symbol appear almost three-dimensional. Color grade across all scenes should be consistent warm-toned with slight desaturation of cool tones and a gentle lift in the amber channel — target the visual aesthetic of early-morning natural light, approximately 4800K color temperature. The cut from scene 3 back to the woman in scene 4 should be a simple hard cut, not a transition — the rhythm should feel conversational. Music should be mixed at 12% volume in scenes 1–3 and fade to 8% in scene 4 so the product shot at the end is not sonically busy. The gold text overlay on the final product shot should use the brand's exact gold (#ab8c52) and appear at 0.5 seconds into the product shot, not at the cut point.

ads.title: Girl Math: The Alex and Ani Edition

storyboard.visual: Direct-to-camera shot. Creator (woman, mid-20s, energetic and warm) looks straight into the lens and raises one finger like she is about to make an important point. She is in a bright, casually styled home setting — natural light from a large window. Her wrist already has two Alex and Ani bangles on it. Expression is playful and knowing.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Creator speaks: 'Okay, girl math time.' Trending 'girl math' audio plays underneath as a recognizable cue, immediately building familiarity. Text overlay in bold white block font top-center: 'GIRL MATH: ALEX AND ANI EDITION'

storyboard.visual: Cut to creator counting on her fingers. Quick-cut rhythm — each 'girl math' point gets its own beat. Her wrist with bangles is visible in the foreground as she gestures. Background stays consistent — same bright home setting.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Creator (fast, playful delivery): 'If I buy a bangle on sale... that is basically free money I saved. If it is eco-conscious recycled metal... that is good for the planet, so it basically cancels out the purchase. If 20% goes to charity... I am basically donating.' Text overlays match each point, appearing in quick succession — white bold uppercase, bottom third.

storyboard.visual: Creator holds up her wrist to the camera showing her existing stack. She then picks up two more bangles from a surface just off-frame — the Starfish Charity Bangle and the Zodiac Charm Bangle — and begins sliding them onto her wrist one at a time on camera. The sliding mechanism is visible.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Creator: 'And if I get my zodiac bangle and my charity bangle during the same sale... I technically only shopped once. Which means... I saved time.' She grins. Text overlay: 'Zodiac Charm Bangle — $38. Starfish Charity Bangle — $36. 20% to ocean conservation.'

storyboard.visual: Creator looks at her completed stack on her wrist — now wearing 4 bangles — then back at camera. She shrugs with a satisfied smile. Cut to a clean product shot of both new bangles on a cream background with sale price callout. CTA bar animates in at the bottom.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Creator: 'Girl math is always correct. Sale link in bio.' End card product shot text overlay: 'Up to 30% off. Made in Rhode Island. Eco-conscious metals.' CTA button: 'Shop Now'

ads.copy: Girl math: buying during the Alex and Ani sale is basically an act of environmental responsibility. You are welcome.
ads.reference: Directly adapted from Mejuri's trend-jacking 'Girl Math is...' Reel format that drove 120 fan response videos in one week and cut CAC by 35%. The translation for Alex and Ani integrates the brand's genuine eco-conscious and Charity by Design USPs into the girl math logic — the humor has factual grounding in real brand claims (recycled metals, 20% to charity), making it more defensible and more shareable than a pure price justification version.
negative_prompts: Do not write the girl math points as generic jokes — each one must reference a real, specific Alex and Ani brand attribute (eco-conscious metals, Charity by Design percentage, one-size-fits-all mechanism) or the concept loses its differentiation.
Avoid casting a creator who delivers the concept in an ironic or detached tone — the girl math format works on warmth and genuine playfulness; deadpan delivery kills the shareability.
No brand logo animation or formal end card — the concept must end on the creator's face or the product shot, not on a corporate sign-off frame.
Avoid using the word 'discount' — the girl math logic reframes value through wit; the word 'discount' breaks the frame and signals a conventional ad.
Do not shoot on a ring light — the flat circular catchlight is visually associated with overly commercial creator content; use natural window light or a softbox positioned to camera side.
ads.post_production_notes: Pacing is the critical production decision in this concept — scenes 1 and 2 should be cut fast (near the rhythm of the trending audio, approximately every 1.5–2 seconds per girl math point) and then scene 3 slows down to let the sliding-on-wrist product moment breathe. The trending audio should be identified at time of production for current cultural relevance — the 'girl math' audio format has multiple iterations; select the one with the highest current Reel usage count at shoot time. Text overlays in scene 2 must appear on cut, not with a delay — the fast-cut rhythm requires text to be synchronized precisely to each verbal point. Export two versions: one with the trending audio prominent (for organic Reel distribution) and one with the trending audio at 15% and a custom music bed at 30% (for paid Meta placement where trending audio may have licensing restrictions).

content_types.name: Stack and Save Promo Video
content_types.hook: Short-form promo videos that make stacking multiple bangles feel like the obvious sale strategy — answering Pandora's BOGO mechanic with Alex and Ani's symbolism-stacking logic.
content_types.why_it_works: Pandora's BOGO 50% off stacking mechanic is documented as their highest-performing sale format because it creates a natural multi-unit purchase loop. BaubleBar's stack-reveal timelapse format drives higher AOV by making completeness feel like the goal. Alex and Ani's expandable one-size-fits-all mechanism is physically designed for stacking — the product itself is the stacking argument — but no current paid creative demonstrates this. This content type creates a branded stacking mechanic that is native to Alex and Ani's product story rather than borrowed from a competitor's discount structure.

ads.title: Build Your Stack in 15 Seconds

storyboard.visual: Overhead shot looking down at a bare wrist resting on a warm cream linen surface. Five Alex and Ani bangles are arranged in a row beside the wrist — the Zodiac Charm Bangle, Path of Life Bangle, Initial Charm Bangle, Rose Gold Feather Bangle, and Infinite Karma Wrap. A hand reaches into frame from above and picks up the first bangle.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Trending upbeat but not aggressive audio — something rhythmic and warm, consistent with a lifestyle aesthetic. No voiceover. Text overlay top of frame, thin white uppercase: 'BUILD YOUR STACK' Sub-text below: 'Sale on now — start at $22'

storyboard.visual: Quick-cut sequence — each bangle being slid onto the wrist gets its own 2-second shot, cut tight on the sliding mechanism in action. Shots alternate between: (a) overhead of bangle approaching wrist, (b) side close-up of the expandable mechanism sliding open, (c) bangle settled into place beside the previous one. Four bangles added in this sequence — 8 seconds total at approximately 2 seconds per bangle.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Each bangle addition syncs to a beat in the audio — the cut lands on the musical pulse. Text overlay updates with each bangle added: bangle name + price in small gold text, bottom left, replacing the previous entry. Running total appears in white bold, bottom right, incrementing with each piece: '$22... $60... $88... $126...'

storyboard.visual: Final wide shot of the complete wrist — all five bangles stacked — raised slightly and rotated slowly. The bangles catch golden-hour window light and each one glints at a slightly different moment as the wrist turns. Camera slowly pushes in toward the stack. Shot is warm, clean, and slightly cinematic — the only moment in this Reel that is not fast-cut.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Audio fades slightly for the final beat. Text overlay: 'Sale ends soon. Every bangle means something different.' CTA button appears at bottom in gold on dark bar: 'SHOP THE STACK — Up to 30% Off'

ads.copy: 5 bangles. 5 meanings. One wrist. Sale is on — build your stack while it lasts.
ads.reference: Adapted from BaubleBar's layer-by-layer stack reveal Reel format and Pandora's BOGO stacking carousel mechanic. The critical Alex and Ani adaptation is using the physical expandable sliding mechanism as the visual proof of the stacking concept — every close-up of the bangle sliding onto the wrist is also a product demo of the patented one-size-fits-all mechanism, turning a promotional format into a feature demonstration simultaneously. The meaning text overlay system (bangle name appearing with each piece) translates Pandora's charm tour narrative structure into a fast-cut visual rhythm.
negative_prompts: Do not use a tripod-locked overhead shot for the entire sequence — the close-up side shots of the mechanism sliding are essential to the product demo value of this concept; a purely overhead format misses the mechanism story.

Avoid a monotone wrist color throughout — for product photography authenticity and brand inclusivity, the casting should show the bangles' contrast and finish quality on warm medium skin tones where the Rafaelian gold reads most clearly in video.

No cartoon or graphic price-counter animation for the running total — the price counter should be clean white system text, incrementing with a simple number change, not with a spinning counter effect that looks cheap.
Avoid syncing every cut to a bass drop or percussive hit — the audio sync should feel rhythmic and confident, not aggressive; the brand tone is warm, not hype.

Do not include more than 5 bangles in the stack for this concept — exceeding 5 pieces makes the wrist look overcrowded and undermines the clear per-bangle meaning structure the text overlays establish.
ads.post_production_notes: The edit should be built in the audio first — lay the selected track and identify the 8 best beat hit points in the 8-second sequence window for cuts 1 through 4 of the bangle-sliding sequence. Then match footage cuts to those points exactly. The running price total in the bottom right should increment on the same frame as each cut — not a half-second delayed. Color grade: warm gold grade with the linen background dialed to approximately 245, 230, 210 in RGB (warm cream, not pure white). The final slow push-in on the complete stack (scene 3) should be achieved optically during the shoot by mounting the camera on a slider moving toward the wrist over 4 seconds — do not use a digital zoom in post as it will degrade the metal texture detail. Export at 1080x1920 for Reel placement and 1080x1080 for square Feed placement as a secondary cut.

ads.title: The $100 Stack That Tells Your Whole Story

storyboard.visual: Close-up handheld shot of a woman (late 20s, casual warm styling — oversized linen shirt) looking at an Alex and Ani bangle she is holding between two fingers, turning it in the light. She is standing by a large window, backlit by soft daylight. She looks at camera and the shot slowly zooms in on her face — not aggressively, just a subtle push. Expression is warm and slightly reflective.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover (warm, conversational female voice): 'I have been building my Alex and Ani stack for three years. But I finally got everything I wanted during one sale.' Ambient background audio — quiet room tone with very low acoustic guitar.

storyboard.visual: Cut to an overhead shot of four bangles laid out on a cream linen surface — each one labeled with a small prop card beneath it (like a museum display): 'Zodiac — Who I Am', 'Path of Life — Where I Am Going', 'Initial — Who I Love', 'Starfish Charity — What I Care About'. A hand reaches in and picks up the Zodiac bangle.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover: 'Each one means something specific to me. My zodiac. My path. My initial. My cause.' Camera stays on the overhead as the hand picks each bangle up briefly, showing it, then sets it back. Text overlay bottom of frame (thin white italic): 'Each symbol. Chosen for a reason.'

storyboard.visual: Cut to close-up of the woman sliding all four bangles onto her wrist one at a time in a single continuous take — no cuts within this scene. The expandable mechanism slides open and shut with each one. When the fourth bangle settles, she rotates her wrist once toward the camera. The stack is complete.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover: 'Four bangles. Under $140 at regular price. Under $100 during the sale.' Text overlay: 'Zodiac Charm Bangle — $38 | Path of Life — $32 | Initial Charm — $28 | Starfish Charity — $36 | Total: $134 regular | $99 on sale now'

storyboard.visual: Woman looks directly at camera, wrist raised to show the stack. Expression shifts from reflective to direct and warm — she is now speaking to the viewer as a peer.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover: 'If you have been thinking about starting your stack — or finishing it — this is the time. Eco-conscious metals. Made in Rhode Island. And they never go out of style.' CTA appears: 'SHOP THE SALE — Bangles from $22' in gold bar bottom of frame.

ads.copy: Four bangles. Four meanings. Under $100 during the sale. Build the stack that tells your whole story.
ads.reference: Adapted from Gorjana's female ambassador brand film format (45% documented sales lift) and Pandora's emotional charm tour Reel structure. The key translation is framing the four-bangle curated stack as a complete personal narrative ('who I am, where I am going, who I love, what I care about') — a story architecture that has no equivalent in any competitor creative and is entirely native to Alex and Ani's 100+ symbol library. The prop label cards (museum display style) are an original execution device that makes the symbolism system tangible and discoverable within a 15-second video.
negative_prompts: Do not use the prop label cards as printed Canva-style graphics — they must look like handwritten or simply printed physical cards placed under the jewelry for the overhead shot; digital overlays will look like an ad template.

Avoid including the Infinite Karma Wrap Bangle in this specific stack — the multi-wrap design sits differently on the wrist than single bangles and would disrupt the visual uniformity of the four-bangle stack shown in scenes 2 and 3.
No upbeat sale music during the brand film sections (scenes 1–3) — the music must stay contemplative and warm until the CTA in scene 4, where a subtle lift in music energy is appropriate.
Do not show a price breakdown that requires mental math — the total calculation must be pre-done and displayed as a single number ('$99 on sale') rather than leaving the viewer to add up four prices.
Avoid a pure white background in any scene — the cream and linen textures are essential to the brand warmth that makes this video feel like a brand film rather than a promotional ad.
ads.post_production_notes: The transition from scene 1 to scene 2 should use a gentle dissolve (8 frames at 24fps) rather than a hard cut — the contemplative tone benefits from a softer editorial rhythm in the brand film sections. Scene 3 must be a single continuous take with no cuts — if the talent struggles with the four-bangle slide in one take, capture multiple takes and select the most natural-feeling one. The text overlay in scene 3 with the price breakdown should appear in two waves: first the bangle names and regular prices, then after 2 seconds the total regular price, then after 1 more second the sale total highlighted in gold — the staggered reveal makes the savings calculation feel like a discovery rather than a data dump. Audio mix: voiceover at 95%, music at 20% for scenes 1–3, music rises to 35% in scene 4 as the CTA appears.

content_types.name: Gifting Problem and Solution Video
content_types.hook: Short empathy-first videos that solve the male gift buyer's anxiety with specific Alex and Ani recommendations — a format with zero competition in the current category.
content_types.why_it_works: Kendra Scott's male-targeted gifting video is one of their documented high-performing formats. The male gift buyer (30–55, Facebook/YouTube, low jewelry knowledge, $40–100 budget) is explicitly identified in Alex and Ani's buyer personas but receives zero dedicated paid creative. Competitor Kendra Scott owns this segment with problem-solution humor — Alex and Ani can enter with the same format but differentiated by the symbolism angle ('not just jewelry — a message') which is more emotionally compelling for a gift buyer who wants to communicate something meaningful. This is a low-competition segment with a clear, documented purchase intent signal.

ads.title: The Guy Who Got It Right

storyboard.visual: A man (early 40s, casual — well-worn flannel or simple button-down, not styled) is seated at a kitchen table with his phone in his hand, scrolling. He looks slightly lost. An open laptop shows a generic jewelry website with too many options. He exhales and sets his phone down. Shot is slightly wide, handheld. Realistic home setting — morning kitchen light.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay at top in white, bold uppercase: 'EVERY GUY IN FEBRUARY.' Soft comedic background audio — nothing loud, just a subtle warm underscore. No voiceover yet.

storyboard.visual: Cut to the man picking up an Alex and Ani bangle — the Crystal Heart Charm Bangle — from a small box on the table. He turns it over in his hands and examines it. His expression shifts from confused to genuinely intrigued. He reads something on his phone (implied: the product's meaning page). He looks up with a quiet 'ah' expression — understanding.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Male voiceover (warm, self-aware, not performatively dumb): 'I found the one thing I actually understood. It is not just a bracelet. The heart charm means you are celebrating a bond. One size fits all so I do not need to know her wrist size. Recycled metal — she cares about that. And $38.' Text overlay mid-frame: 'Crystal Heart Charm Bangle — $38. No wrist measurement needed.'

storyboard.visual: Cut to a woman (his partner, implied) opening a small Alex and Ani gift box at the same kitchen table. She lifts out the Crystal Heart Charm Bangle and puts it on. The expandable mechanism slides onto her wrist. She smiles — genuinely, not performatively. He watches with obvious relief and satisfaction. Shot stays warm and real — not a commercial romance scene, just a human moment.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover continues: 'She loved it. Because it actually meant something. Not because I spent a lot.' Soft audio lifts slightly. Text overlay bottom: 'Made in Rhode Island. Eco-conscious metals. $38 — on sale now.'

storyboard.visual: Cut to a clean product shot — the Crystal Heart Charm Bangle on a cream linen surface in warm light. Sale price overlay animates in. A second bangle appears beside it — the Initial Charm Bangle — with a second price overlay. The frame settles with both products visible and a CTA bar at the bottom.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Voiceover: 'Alex and Ani. Gifts that actually mean something. Sale on now — bangles from $22.' CTA bar: 'SHOP GIFTS FOR HER — Sale From $22' in gold on dark background. URL or shop link shown.

ads.copy: She does not want another candle. One size fits all, no wrist measurement needed. Meaningful gifts from $22 — sale on now.
ads.reference: Directly adapted from Kendra Scott's male gift buyer problem-solution video format. The critical translation for Alex and Ani is replacing Kendra Scott's humor-forward approach with a warmer, more empathetic register — the man is not played as a buffoon but as someone genuinely trying to give a meaningful gift, which aligns with Alex and Ani's empowering, non-condescending brand voice. The USP integration (one size fits all, eco-conscious metals, meaning behind every symbol) gives the male buyer three distinct confidence anchors that Kendra Scott's format does not provide.
negative_prompts: Do not cast the male character as comedically incompetent or foolish — Alex and Ani's brand voice is empowering and inclusive, not humorous at anyone's expense; condescending male-gift-buyer tropes will alienate both the male viewer and female secondary viewers.

Avoid showing a gift-wrapping sequence or bow-and-ribbon presentation — the emotional authenticity of the concept depends on the moment feeling real and domestic, not like a commercial gift advertisement.
No stock footage of jewelry stores or mall settings — this must be shot entirely in a real home environment; any commercial setting will break the relatable authenticity the concept requires.

Avoid voiceover delivery that sounds scripted or professionally produced — the male voice should be warm and conversational, with slight imperfection; a studio-clean read will undermine the genuine feel.

Do not include more than two products in the final product shot — the male gift buyer is decision-fatigued; showing too many options in the CTA frame reintroduces the overwhelm the entire concept is working to resolve.
ads.post_production_notes: The entire concept should be shot in a single real home location — do not switch locations between scenes 1 and 3, as visual continuity of the kitchen setting is essential to the domestic narrative coherence. Scene 2 requires a practical prop: the man should be shown actually reading from his phone, which means the art department needs to set up an Alex and Ani product page on the phone visible in the frame (use alexandani.com's actual charm meaning page for the Crystal Heart). Color grade: warm domestic tones — reduce cool shadows, lift amber midtones to approximately +15 in Lightroom-equivalent scale. The scene 3 reaction shot of the woman sliding on the bangle must be captured in a single take — do not cut within this moment, as editing out any frames will disrupt the genuine emotional read. For Facebook placement, export a square (1:1) crop version with the text overlays repositioned; for YouTube pre-roll, extend scene 1 to 5 seconds to account for the skip threshold.

ads.title: Pick Her Symbol — The 3-Step Gift Guide

storyboard.visual: Full-frame static image in portrait orientation (4:5 crop for Meta Feed). Warm cream background. Three Alex and Ani bangles arranged in a clean horizontal row from left to right: the Zodiac Charm Bangle (for 'who she is'), the Path of Life Charm Bangle (for 'where she is going'), and the Starfish Charity Bangle (for 'what she cares about'). Each bangle has a thin hairline bracket label above it in small uppercase serif text indicating the category. A bold serif headline at the top of the frame: 'PICK HER SYMBOL.' Subline below in thin sans-serif: '3 steps to a gift she will actually wear every day.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Static image. Primary text (ad copy): 'She already knows what she believes in. Now you do too. Alex and Ani — meaningful gifts from $22. Sale on now.' Headline overlay on image: 'PICK HER SYMBOL' in gold serif.

storyboard.visual:
Below the three product images, a clean three-step text block in the lower third of the frame:
  Step 1: 'What is her zodiac sign? — We have it.' (with a small zodiac bangle icon)
  Step 2: 'What does she value? — Spirituality, adventure, family, cause.' (with a path of life icon)
  Step 3: 'What is her budget? — Bangles from $22 to $48.' (with a price indicator)
All steps are in thin readable sans-serif. A gold CTA button at the bottom: 'SHOP GIFTS FOR HER — Sale On Now'. Small fine print below button: 'One size fits all. No wrist measurement needed. Free shipping over $50.'
storyboard.audio_or_text: Static image — no audio. The three-step structure is the entire communicative device. The 'no wrist measurement needed' line addresses the male gift buyer's primary sizing anxiety explicitly.

ads.copy: She already knows what she believes in. Pick her symbol. Meaningful gifts from $22 — sale on now. No wrist measurement needed.
ads.reference: Adapted from Kendra Scott's product-forward sale static format and Pandora's Gifts Under $50 decision-simplification carousel structure, compressed into a single static frame designed for Facebook Feed where the male gift buyer audience skews highest. The three-step gift guide framework is original to this execution — no competitor currently runs a decision-tree gift guide as a static ad unit in the jewelry category, making this format proprietary to Alex and Ani in the category's current paid landscape.
negative_prompts: Do not use a script font for any of the three-step labels — legibility is paramount for a male audience on mobile Facebook Feed where font rendering is compressed; use clean sans-serif throughout the step structure.

Avoid including more than three bangles in the product row — decision paralysis is the primary barrier for the male gift buyer persona; the three-bangle curation mirrors the three-step framework and must not be expanded.

No pink or rose-gold dominant color palette for this specific static — the male gift buyer's engagement drops with heavily gendered color coding on the first impression; the cream and gold palette is more neutral while still feeling premium and warm.

Do not show packaging, gift boxes, or wrapping in this image — the static communicates the product itself as the gift, not the packaging ritual; packaging imagery shifts the focus from product meaning to commercial gifting logistics.

Avoid using stock photography for the bangle product images — all three products must be shot in-house with consistent lighting and background, as stock imagery will have color temperature variations that make the horizontal row look mismatched.
ads.post_production_notes: This static must be produced in three crop formats: 4:5 portrait for Meta Feed (primary), 1:1 square for Instagram Feed secondary placement, and 9:16 vertical for Story placement with the step structure repositioned below a full-frame product image. The three bangles in the horizontal product row must all be shot in the same session on the same surface with the same lighting to ensure color temperature and shadow consistency across the row — even a 200K color temperature difference between the three shots will be visible at ad scale. The hairline bracket labels above each bangle should be 0.5pt line weight in #ab8c52 (brand gold) — heavier lines will overwhelm the product imagery. For Facebook Feed placement, the ad headline field (not the image overlay) should read: 'The gift she will actually wear. Alex and Ani — sale from $22.' This keeps primary text clean while the image carries the decision framework.

ad_concepts.production_notes: All creative across this campaign should maintain a consistent warm-golden visual signature — shoot everything in natural daylight or matched daylight-equivalent artificial light at approximately 5000–5200K, with amber channels lifted slightly in post to reinforce the Rafaelian gold brand palette without color-correcting the metal itself. Music direction should vary by content type but stay within a unified tonal family: lo-fi indie acoustic for UGC Nostalgia content, quiet ambient piano for Meaning-Driven Emotional Reels, rhythmic warm electronic for Stack and Save Promo Videos, and understated domestic underscore for Gifting Problem and Solution content — all tracks should sit at 15–25% in the mix beneath voiceover so the brand voice (human or narrator) is always the primary audio element. Visual language across all formats prioritizes the bangle on a real wrist over flat lay product shots wherever possible, because the patented expandable sliding mechanism is Alex and Ani's most demonstrable physical differentiator and should be shown in use in at least one scene of every video format produced.
